key_insight: A frontier language model constrained to explicit reaction-network reasoning can generate experimentally testable catalyst-selectivity hypotheses and reveal controllable levers for CO2 electroreduction.
engineering_relevance: This enables mechanism-guided catalyst design that can steer product selectivity, demonstrated by a copper-iron oxide catalyst with about 3× higher acetate selectivity than Cu-rich baselines.
method: Enforced network invariance so the model reasons over reaction graphs rather than static descriptors.
Mapped pathway competition in CO2 electroreduction to identify ketene desorption, hydroxide capture, and adsorbed CO/CH2 coupling routes.
Translated the inferred levers into synthesis guidance: local alkalinity, iron incorporation, and limited proton-donor access.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports prospective experimental validation, where the model-guided copper-iron oxide catalyst achieved a threefold increase in acetate selectivity versus matched Cu-rich controls.
watch_item: The approach depends on accurate reaction-network specification and careful constraint design, so incomplete mechanistic graphs could lead to misleading hypotheses.
paper_title: Reaction-network reasoning with frontier models for experimentally confirmed catalyst-selectivity hypotheses
